hero.title: 🫁 Physiology
hero.subtitle: The intuitive mind is a sacred gift and the rational mind is a faithful servant - Albert Einstein

topics.content:
• Endocrine vs Exocrine: Endocrine = hormones into blood (distant effect); Exocrine = ducts → local action (enzymes, sweat).
  • Hormone Types: Peptides (water-soluble), Steroids (cholesterol-derived, lipid-soluble), Amines (mixed behavior).
  • Water-Soluble Hormones: Stored in vesicles, circulate free, bind cell surface receptors → act via 2nd messengers (FAST).
  • Lipid-Soluble Hormones: Synthesized on demand, travel bound to proteins, bind intracellular receptors → alter gene transcription (SLOW).
  • Peptide Hormone Synthesis: Preprohormone → Prohormone (ER/Golgi) → Stored in vesicles → Released by exocytosis.
  • Steroid Hormones: Derived from cholesterol (4-ring structure) → diffuse across membranes → no storage.
  • Transport: Water-soluble = free in plasma; Steroids/thyroid = bound to carrier proteins (inactive reservoir, longer half-life).
  • Half-Life: Peptides = short (minutes); Steroids/thyroid = long (hours–days).
  • Receptors: Water-soluble = membrane receptors; Lipid-soluble = intracellular (cytoplasm/nucleus).
  • Negative Feedback: Free hormone levels regulate secretion → maintains homeostasis.
  • Major Glands: Hypothalamus, Pituitary (master), Thyroid, Parathyroid, Adrenal, Pancreas, Gonads.
  • Functions: Regulates metabolism, growth, reproduction, blood pressure, electrolytes, behavior.

• Pituitary Location: Lies in the sella turcica at the base of the brain and is connected to the hypothalamus via the infundibulum (pituitary stalk).
  • Divisions: Anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis = glandular), Posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis = neural), Pars intermedia (minimal in humans).
  • Embryology: Anterior pituitary develops from Rathke’s pouch (oral ectoderm); Posterior pituitary develops from neuroectoderm of the hypothalamus.
  • Posterior Pituitary: Stores and releases ADH and Oxytocin (produced in hypothalamus) via axonal transport (magnocellular neurons).
  • ADH Function: Increases water reabsorption in kidneys → concentrates urine.
  • Oxytocin Function: Causes uterine contraction and milk ejection (let-down reflex).
  • Anterior Pituitary Control: Regulated by hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones via the hypothalamic–hypophyseal portal system.
  • Portal System: Specialized vascular system connecting hypothalamus to anterior pituitary for rapid hormone delivery.
  • Hypothalamic Hormones: TRH (↑TSH), CRH (↑ACTH), GHRH (↑GH), Somatostatin (↓GH), GnRH (↑FSH/LH), Dopamine (↓Prolactin).
  • Dopamine: Key inhibitor of prolactin secretion (Prolactin-Inhibiting Hormone).
  • Anterior Pituitary Cells: Somatotropes (GH), Corticotropes (ACTH), Thyrotropes (TSH), Gonadotropes (FSH/LH), Lactotropes (Prolactin).
  • Most Abundant Cells: Somatotropes (~30–40% of anterior pituitary).
  • Growth Hormone (GH): Acts via JAK-STAT pathway and through IGF-1 (somatomedin) to promote growth.
  • GH Effects: Increases cell size, mitosis, protein synthesis, and bone growth.
  • GH Disorders: Excess in children → Gigantism; Excess in adults → Acromegaly; Deficiency → Dwarfism.
  • Posterior vs Anterior: Posterior = neural control & hormone storage; Anterior = endocrine gland regulated by blood-borne signals.
  • Median Eminence: Interface where hypothalamic hormones enter portal circulation.
  • Key Concept: Hypothalamus controls pituitary → pituitary controls peripheral endocrine glands (master regulator system).

• Pancreatic Islets: Clusters of endocrine cells (Islets of Langerhans) responsible for glucose homeostasis.
  • Cell Types: Alpha (glucagon), Beta (insulin, amylin), Delta (somatostatin), PP cells (pancreatic polypeptide).
  • Distribution: Beta cells (~60–80%) central; Alpha cells (~20–30%) peripheral.
  • Insulin Structure: Peptide hormone with A and B chains connected by disulfide bonds; derived from proinsulin.
  • C-Peptide: Released in equimolar amounts with insulin; used to assess endogenous insulin production.
  • Insulin Storage: Stored in granules as a crystalline complex with zinc.
  • Insulin Secretion Trigger: Increased blood glucose → ↑ ATP → closure of ATP-sensitive K+ channels → depolarization → Ca2+ influx → exocytosis.
  • Sulfonylureas: Stimulate insulin release by blocking ATP-sensitive K+ channels.
  • Insulin Receptor: Tyrosine kinase receptor with α (binding) and β (kinase) subunits.
  • Mechanism of Action: Autophosphorylation → activation of IRS → metabolic and gene transcription effects.
  • Immediate Effects: GLUT4 translocation → ↑ glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue.
  • Intermediate Effects: Enzyme phosphorylation → metabolic changes.
  • Long-Term Effects: Gene transcription → protein synthesis and growth.
  • GLUT Transporters: GLUT4 (insulin-dependent), GLUT2 (pancreas/liver sensor), GLUT1/3 (brain/RBCs, insulin-independent).
  • Insulin Effects on Carbohydrates: ↑ glucose uptake, ↑ glycogenesis, ↓ gluconeogenesis.
  • Insulin Effects on Lipids: ↑ lipogenesis, ↓ lipolysis, ↓ ketogenesis.
  • Insulin Effects on Protein: ↑ amino acid uptake, ↑ protein synthesis, ↓ proteolysis.
  • Electrolyte Effects: ↑ K+, Mg2+, phosphate uptake into cells.
  • Hormone of Plenty: Insulin is secreted in the fed state to promote storage of nutrients.
  • Regulation: Stimulated by glucose, amino acids, incretins (GLP-1, GIP), parasympathetic activity; inhibited by sympathetic activity.
  • Glucagon: Secreted by alpha cells; acts via cAMP to increase blood glucose.
  • Glucagon Effects: ↑ glycogenolysis, ↑ gluconeogenesis, ↑ lipolysis.
  • Target Organ: Liver is the primary target for glucagon.
  • Insulin vs Glucagon: Insulin lowers blood glucose; glucagon raises it.
  • Fed State: ↑ insulin, ↓ glucagon → storage of nutrients.
  • Fasting State: ↓ insulin, ↑ glucagon → mobilization of energy stores.
  • Insulinoma: High insulin + high C-peptide → endogenous overproduction.
  • Exogenous Insulin Use: High insulin + low C-peptide.
  • Disulfide Bonds: Essential for insulin activity; disruption leads to inactive hormone.
  • Growth Effects: Insulin promotes IGF-1 production and works synergistically with GH.
  • Fetal Effects: Insulin deficiency → low birth weight; excess → macrosomia.
  • Antagonistic Hormones: GH and cortisol oppose insulin effects.
  • Key Concept: Insulin is the main anabolic hormone that promotes storage, while glucagon is catabolic and mobilizes energy.

• Structure: Adrenal gland has two main parts: cortex (outer, steroid hormones) and medulla (inner, catecholamines).
  • Cortical Zones (GFR): Zona Glomerulosa (mineralocorticoids), Zona Fasciculata (glucocorticoids), Zona Reticularis (androgens).
  • Zona Glomerulosa: Secretes aldosterone → regulates Na+, K+, and water balance.
  • Zona Fasciculata: Secretes cortisol → regulates metabolism, stress response.
  • Zona Reticularis: Produces adrenal androgens (DHEA, androstenedione).
  • Medulla: Secretes catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine) for fight-or-flight response.
  • Steroid Hormone Origin: All adrenal steroids are derived from cholesterol.
  • Steroid Classes: C21 (glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids), C19 (androgens), C18 (estrogens).
  • Rate-Limiting Step: Conversion of cholesterol → pregnenolone (stimulated by ACTH).
  • ACTH Role: Stimulates cortisol and androgen secretion from adrenal cortex.
  • RAAS System: Regulates aldosterone via angiotensin II and plasma potassium.
  • Aldosterone Stimuli: ↑ K+, ↓ Na+, ↓ blood volume → ↑ aldosterone secretion.
  • Aldosterone Action: ↑ Na+ reabsorption, ↑ K+ and H+ secretion → metabolic alkalosis.
  • Angiotensin II Mechanism: Acts via Gq receptors → IP3/Ca2+ pathway.
  • ANP Effect: Inhibits aldosterone secretion.
  • Cortisol Transport: Mostly bound to cortisol-binding globulin (transcortin) and albumin.
  • Aldosterone Transport: Less protein-bound → more free hormone.
  • Diurnal Rhythm: Cortisol peaks in morning (~8 AM), lowest at midnight.
  • Pulsatile Secretion: ACTH and cortisol released in bursts every 2–3 hours.
  • Cortisol Functions: ↑ gluconeogenesis, ↑ blood glucose, promotes survival in fasting.
  • Carbohydrate Effects: Maintains glucose levels, increases glycogen stores.
  • Lipid Effects: ↑ lipolysis → ↑ free fatty acids.
  • Protein Effects: ↑ protein breakdown → muscle wasting in excess.
  • Permissive Effects: Cortisol enhances actions of glucagon and catecholamines.
  • Anti-inflammatory: Cortisol suppresses immune and inflammatory responses.
  • Immunosuppressive: Prevents excessive immune activation.
  • Bone Effects: ↓ bone formation, ↑ bone resorption → osteoporosis.
  • Connective Tissue: ↓ fibroblast activity → thin skin, fragile vessels.
  • GI Effects: ↑ gastric acid and pepsin → risk of ulcers.
  • Renal Effects: ↑ renal blood flow and GFR; weak mineralocorticoid effect.
  • Neuro Effects: Alters mood, memory, and sleep (excess → insomnia).
  • Electrolyte Effect: Cortisol has weak Na+ retention via mineralocorticoid receptors.
  • 11β-HSD Function: Converts cortisol → cortisone to protect mineralocorticoid receptors.
  • 17-OH Steroids: Urinary marker of cortisol secretion.
  • 17-Ketosteroids: Reflect androgen production.
  • Stress Response: Cortisol increases during trauma, infection, hypoglycemia, and exercise.
  • Hypothalamic Control: CRH → ACTH → adrenal cortex (HPA axis).
  • Negative Feedback: Cortisol inhibits CRH and ACTH release.
  • Mineralocorticoid Potency: Aldosterone accounts for ~90% of activity.
  • Key Concept: Adrenal cortex hormones regulate metabolism, electrolytes, and stress, while medulla controls acute stress responses.

1_intro.q: Which of the following best describes an endocrine gland?
a: Secretes substances through ducts to a surface
Releases hormones directly into the bloodstream
Acts only locally at site of release
1_intro.l: Endocrine glands release hormones directly into the bloodstream to act on distant targets.

1_intro.q: Which of the following is an example of an exocrine function of the pancreas?
1_intro.l: The exocrine pancreas secretes digestive enzymes into ducts leading to the intestine.

1_intro.q: Which hormone type is most likely stored in secretory vesicles?
1_intro.l: Water-soluble peptide hormones are synthesized and stored in vesicles for rapid release.

1_intro.q: Which hormone type is synthesized on demand rather than stored?
1_intro.l: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and synthesized when needed from cholesterol.

1_intro.q: Which of the following is TRUE about water-soluble hormones?
1_intro.l: Water-soluble hormones bind surface receptors and act via second messenger systems.

1_intro.q: Which of the following is TRUE about lipid-soluble hormones?
a: They act via cell surface receptors
1_intro.l: Lipid-soluble hormones enter cells and alter gene transcription, leading to protein synthesis.

1_intro.q: Which hormone class includes thyroid hormones?
1_intro.l: Thyroid hormones are amines but behave like lipid-soluble hormones.

1_intro.q: Where are peptide hormones synthesized?
1_intro.l: Peptide hormones are synthesized in the rough ER as preprohormones.

1_intro.q: What is the first step in peptide hormone synthesis?
1_intro.l: Peptide hormones begin as preprohormones before being processed into prohormones.

1_intro.q: Which organelle converts preprohormone to prohormone?
1_intro.l: The ER cleaves preprohormone into prohormone.

1_intro.q: Where is prohormone packaged into vesicles?
1_intro.l: The Golgi apparatus packages prohormones into secretory vesicles.

1_intro.q: What triggers peptide hormone release?
1_intro.l: Peptide hormones are released via exocytosis when vesicles fuse with the membrane.

1_intro.q: Which of the following hormones is derived from cholesterol?
1_intro.l: Steroid hormones like cortisol are synthesized from cholesterol.

1_intro.q: Which gland produces cortisol?
1_intro.l: Cortisol is produced by the adrenal cortex.

1_intro.q: Which structure is part of steroid hormone backbone?
1_intro.l: Steroid hormones share a four-ring cholesterol backbone.

1_intro.q: Which hormone travels mostly bound in plasma?
1_intro.l: Thyroid hormone is mostly bound to carrier proteins in blood.

1_intro.q: What is the function of carrier proteins?
1_intro.l: Carrier proteins increase hormone stability and prolong half-life.

1_intro.q: Which hormone has the shortest half-life?
1_intro.l: Peptide hormones have short half-lives (minutes).

1_intro.q: Where do lipid-soluble hormones bind receptors?
1_intro.l: Lipid-soluble hormones bind intracellular receptors.

1_intro.q: Which system works with endocrine system to maintain homeostasis?
1_intro.l: Endocrine and nervous systems coordinate to regulate homeostasis.

1_intro.q: Which of the following is regulated by hormones?
1_intro.l: Hormones regulate metabolism, reproduction, blood pressure, and more.

1_intro.q: Which gland is considered the master endocrine gland?
1_intro.l: The pituitary gland controls other endocrine glands.

1_intro.q: Which hormone is produced in the pancreas?
1_intro.l: The pancreas produces insulin from beta cells.

1_intro.q: What is C-peptide?
1_intro.l: C-peptide is released when proinsulin is converted into insulin.

1_intro.q: What maintains hormone levels in the body?
1_intro.l: Negative feedback regulates hormone levels to maintain balance.

1_intro.q: Which form of hormone provides negative feedback?
1_intro.l: The free (active) hormone level controls feedback mechanisms.

1_intro.q: Which of the following describes hormone action speed?
1_intro.l: Peptides act fast via second messengers; steroids act slowly via gene transcription.

1_intro.q: Which gland produces aldosterone?
1_intro.l: Aldosterone is a steroid hormone produced by the adrenal cortex.

1_intro.q: Which of the following hormones can diffuse through cell membranes?
1_intro.l: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and diffuse through membranes.

1_intro.q: Which of the following best describes endocrine signaling?
1_intro.l: Endocrine hormones travel through blood to distant target organs.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: The pituitary gland is located in which anatomical structure?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: The pituitary sits in the sella turcica at the base of the brain.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: The anterior pituitary develops from which embryologic structure?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Anterior pituitary arises from Rathke’s pouch (oral ectoderm).

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: The posterior pituitary is derived from:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Posterior pituitary originates as neural outgrowth from hypothalamus.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which cell type is found in the posterior pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Posterior pituitary contains glial-like cells called pituicytes.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which of the following hormones is produced in the hypothalamus but released from the posterior pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: ADH is synthesized in hypothalamus and released from posterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Oxytocin primarily acts on which organs?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Oxytocin causes uterine contraction and milk ejection.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: ADH primarily affects which organ?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: ADH increases water reabsorption in kidneys.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which neurons synthesize ADH and oxytocin?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Magnocellular neurons synthesize ADH and oxytocin.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Hormones of the posterior pituitary reach the gland via:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: They travel via axons from hypothalamus.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which system regulates the anterior pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Anterior pituitary is regulated by hypothalamic portal system.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: The hypothalamic–hypophyseal portal system connects:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Portal system carries releasing hormones to anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which hormone inhibits prolactin release?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Dopamine acts as prolactin-inhibiting hormone (PIH).

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: GHRH stimulates secretion of:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Growth hormone releasing hormone stimulates GH release.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Somatostatin primarily inhibits:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Somatostatin inhibits GH release.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: CRH stimulates release of:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: CRH stimulates ACTH from anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: GnRH stimulates secretion of:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: GnRH stimulates FSH and LH.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which anterior pituitary cells secrete GH?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Somatotropes secrete growth hormone.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: ACTH is secreted by which cells?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Corticotropes secrete ACTH.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: TSH is secreted by:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Thyrotropes produce TSH.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: FSH and LH are secreted by:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Gonadotropes produce FSH and LH.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Prolactin is secreted by:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Lactotropes produce prolactin.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which anterior pituitary cells are most abundant?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Somatotropes make up ~30–40% of anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Growth hormone acts primarily via:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: GH signals via JAK2-STAT pathway.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which mediator carries out many effects of GH?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: IGF-1 mediates GH effects.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Growth hormone primarily promotes:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: GH increases cell size, mitosis, and differentiation.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which condition results from GH excess in childhood?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: GH excess before epiphyseal closure → gigantism.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: GH excess in adults leads to:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Acromegaly occurs after epiphyseal closure.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: GH deficiency in childhood results in:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Low GH → short stature (dwarfism).

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which structure connects hypothalamus to pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Infundibulum (pituitary stalk) connects them.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: The median eminence is part of:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Median eminence links hypothalamus to pituitary portal system.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Anterior pituitary secretion is controlled by:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Controlled by hypothalamic hormones via portal system.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Posterior pituitary secretion is controlled by:
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Posterior pituitary is controlled by nerve signals.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which hormone increases water reabsorption in kidneys?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: ADH increases water reabsorption in collecting ducts.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which hormone causes milk ejection reflex?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Oxytocin causes milk ejection.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: Which hormone is responsible for milk production?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Prolactin stimulates milk production.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A nursing mother notes that her milk begins to flow just moments after she hears her infant cry in the next room, even before the baby begins to suckle. This milk ejection reflex is mediated by oxytocin causing the contraction of which of the following cells?
a: Smooth muscle of the lactiferous ducts
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Oxytocin causes contraction of myoepithelial cells to eject milk.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A 68-year-old male is brought to the emergency department after three days of severe vomiting and diarrhea. On examination, he is lethargic and has a blood pressure of 80/45 mmHg and a heart rate of 125 bpm. Laboratory studies reveal that his plasma osmolarity is slightly lower than normal due to the ingestion of large amounts of plain water during his illness. Despite the low osmolarity, his plasma antidiuretic hormone (ADH) levels are found to be 40 times the normal reference range. Which of the following best explains why ADH remains elevated despite the patient's hypo-osmolar state?
a: Activation of osmoreceptors in the organum vasculosum.
Decreased stretch of baroreceptors overriding osmotic inhibition.
Increased sensitivity of the V2 receptors in the collecting ducts.
Direct stimulation of the supraoptic nuclei by atrial natriuretic peptide.
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Severe hypovolemia activates baroreceptors which overrides osmotic control of ADH.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A researcher is investigating the growth-promoting effects of growth hormone (GH) in a canine model. In one experimental group, the animals have undergone a total pancreatectomy. Despite receiving daily injections of high-dose recombinant growth hormone, these animals show no significant increase in lean body mass or bone growth. Based on the physiological requirements of GH, which of the following is most likely responsible for the lack of growth in these animals?
a: Deficiency of the JAK2-STAT signaling pathway.
Excessive mobilization of free fatty acids causing ketosis.
Reduced half-life of growth hormone in the absence of pancreatic enzymes.
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: GH requires insulin for IGF-1 mediated growth; without insulin, growth is impaired.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A researcher is studying the development of the endocrine system. They identify a structure that invaginates from the pharyngeal epithelium and eventually loses its connection to the oral cavity. This structure, known as Rathke's pouch, will eventually give rise to which of the following?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Rathke’s pouch forms the anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis).

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A 45-year-old patient suffers a traumatic injury to the median eminence of the hypothalamus. While the posterior pituitary function remains partially intact due to the proximity of neuronal cell bodies, the regulation of the anterior pituitary is severely compromised. Which of the following delivery mechanisms for hypothalamic factors is primarily impaired in this patient?
a: Axoplasmic flow via the hypothalamic-hypophysial tract
Direct neural stimulation of glandular cells
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Anterior pituitary hormones are regulated via hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal circulation.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A 48-year-old male with an acidophilic adenoma of the anterior pituitary presents with fatigue and increased thirst. Laboratory tests reveal a fasting blood glucose of 145 mg/dL. Which of the following best describes the metabolic effect of his condition on glucose utilization?
a: Increased glucose uptake in skeletal muscle
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Excess GH decreases glucose uptake and increases blood glucose → insulin resistance.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A clinical study investigates factors that inhibit the release of GH. It is found that a specific hypothalamic hormone exerts a powerful inhibitory effect on somatotropes. Which of the following substances primarily inhibits GH secretion by blocking the action of Growth Hormone-Releasing Hormone (GHRH)?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Somatostatin inhibits GH release from the anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A 52-year-old patient presents with a protruding lower jaw (prognathism), enlarged hands requiring size 14 gloves, and a hunched back (kyphosis). If these symptoms resulted from an acidophilic tumor, which of the following is most likely true regarding the onset of the excess hormone secretion?
a: It began before the fusion of the long bone epiphyses.
It began after the fusion of the long bone epiphyses.
It was caused by a deficiency in IGF-1.
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Acromegaly occurs after epiphyseal plate closure.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A patient with central diabetes insipidus is administered a synthetic analog of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) to reduce their excessive urine output. This medication acts on V2 receptors to increase the water permeability of the collecting ducts by stimulating the insertion of which of the following?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: ADH inserts aquaporin-2 channels in collecting ducts to reabsorb water.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A patient is brought to the emergency room following a major motor vehicle accident with an estimated blood loss of 25% of their total volume. Which of the following is the most potent driver of the resulting high levels of ADH secretion in this patient?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Hypovolemia is the strongest stimulus for ADH release.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A 32-year-old woman experienced severe circulatory shock due to massive hemorrhage during the delivery of her child. Several months later, she reports an inability to lactate, loss of libido, and significant lethargy. What is the most likely underlying cause of her panhypopituitarism?
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Sheehan syndrome is caused by ischemic necrosis of the pituitary due to postpartum hemorrhage.

2_pituitary_gland_v1.q: A group of children is found to have significantly short stature despite having normal or even elevated plasma concentrations of growth hormone. This condition, seen in Laron syndrome, is most likely due to which of the following?
a: Generalized deficiency of anterior pituitary secretion
Inability to form insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1)
2_pituitary_gland_v1.l: Laron syndrome is due to GH receptor defect leading to decreased IGF-1 production.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following best describes the pituitary gland?
a: A peripheral endocrine gland located in the thyroid bed
The master gland located at the base of the brain
A part of the adrenal medulla that regulates stress hormones
A duct-containing gland located in the oral cavity
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The pituitary gland is described as the master gland and is located at the base of the brain.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The pituitary gland sits in which of the following structures?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The pituitary gland is located in the sella turcica, a small cavity in the sphenoid bone.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which structure is located directly above the pituitary gland?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The optic chiasm lies above the pituitary gland.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The pituitary gland is connected to the hypothalamus by the:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The pituitary stalk connects the pituitary gland to the hypothalamus.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The pituitary gland connects to which specific hypothalamic region?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The pituitary connects to the median eminence of the hypothalamus.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The pituitary gland is considered one of the circumventricular organs because it:
a: Does not contain the blood-brain barrier
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The pituitary is one of the circumventricular organs that does not contain the blood-brain barrier.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The posterior pituitary is also known as the:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The posterior pituitary is called the neurohypophysis.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which hormones are released from the posterior pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The posterior pituitary releases ADH (vasopressin) and oxytocin.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The posterior pituitary is embryologically derived from:
a: Neural ectoderm from the floor of the forebrain
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The posterior pituitary is derived from neural ectoderm in the floor of the forebrain.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following best describes the tissue composition of the posterior pituitary?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The posterior pituitary contains axons and nerve terminals from hypothalamic neurons.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The neurons that release posterior pituitary hormones originate in the:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Posterior pituitary hormones are synthesized by neurons that originate in the hypothalamus.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which hypothalamic nucleus is most associated with oxytocin production?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The paraventricular nucleus is associated with oxytocin production.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which hypothalamic nucleus is most associated with ADH production?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The supraoptic nucleus is associated with ADH production.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The anterior pituitary is also called the:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The anterior pituitary is called the adenohypophysis.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The anterior pituitary develops from:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The anterior pituitary is derived from Rathke's pouch, an outgrowth of the oral cavity.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Rathke's pouch is an outgrowth of the:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Rathke's pouch is an outgrowth of the oral cavity.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which anterior pituitary cell type secretes ACTH?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Corticotrophs secrete ACTH.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which anterior pituitary cell type secretes TSH?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Thyrotrophs secrete TSH.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which anterior pituitary cell type secretes LH and FSH?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Gonadotrophs secrete LH and FSH.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which anterior pituitary cell type secretes growth hormone?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Somatotrophs secrete growth hormone.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which anterior pituitary cell type secretes prolactin?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Lactotrophs secrete prolactin.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The main blood supply delivering hypothalamic releasing and inhibiting hormones to the anterior pituitary is the:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The hypothalamic portal system is the main blood supply to the anterior pituitary and carries regulatory hormones.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which hypothalamic hormone inhibits prolactin release?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Dopamine inhibits prolactin release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Somatostatin released from the hypothalamus inhibits which of the following pituitary hormones?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Somatostatin inhibits GH and TSH.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: CRH from the hypothalamus stimulates release of:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: CRH stimulates ACTH release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: TRH from the hypothalamus stimulates release of:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: TRH stimulates TSH release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: GnRH stimulates release of which pituitary hormones?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: GnRH stimulates LH and FSH release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: GHRH stimulates the release of:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: GHRH stimulates growth hormone release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following best describes prolactin?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Prolactin is a protein hormone.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Prolactin is primarily under what type of hypothalamic control?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Prolactin is under inhibitory control from the hypothalamus, mainly by dopamine.
2_pituitary_gland_v2.h: Unlike many others, it is mainly inhibited

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: An increase in dopamine release would most likely cause which of the following?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Increased dopamine reduces prolactin release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following can stimulate prolactin release?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: TRH can stimulate prolactin release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which endocrine disorder predisposes to hyperprolactinemia?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Hypothyroidism predisposes to hyperprolactinemia.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Hypothyroidism should be considered in the differential diagnosis of:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Hypothyroidism is part of the differential for pituitary enlargement and hyperprolactinemia.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which hormone stimulates prolactin release and contributes to lactotroph hyperplasia in pregnancy?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Estrogen stimulates prolactin release and there is a marked increase in lactotrophs during pregnancy.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: During pregnancy, prolactin inhibits the release of:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Prolactin inhibits GnRH release during pregnancy.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: One major physiologic action of prolactin is to:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Prolactin stimulates growth of mammary glands.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The most common secreting tumor of the anterior pituitary is a:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: A prolactinoma is the most common secreting tumor in the anterior pituitary.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: An adenoma of lactotrophs leads to:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Adenoma of lactotrophs causes hyperlactinemia.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: An adenoma of corticotrophs leads to:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Adenoma of corticotrophs leads to Cushing's disease.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following best describes hormone flow to the posterior pituitary?
a: Hormones are synthesized in hypothalamic magnocellular neurons and transported down axons to be released into the bloodstream
Hormones are made in the posterior pituitary and sent to the hypothalamus
Hormones are secreted into the portal system and then modified by the posterior pituitary
Hormones are produced in pituicytes and stored in the thalamus
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Posterior pituitary hormones are synthesized in hypothalamic magnocellular neurons, transported via axons, and released into blood.
2_pituitary_gland_v2.h: Posterior pituitary stores and releases, does not synthesize

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following best describes hormone flow to the anterior pituitary?
a: Hormones travel by axons directly into blood
Hypothalamic releasing and inhibiting hormones enter the hypophyseal portal system and regulate anterior pituitary cells
The anterior pituitary sends hormones back to the hypothalamus for activation
Hormones are secreted only through cerebrospinal fluid
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Parvocellular hypothalamic neurons release regulatory hormones into the hypophyseal portal system to control anterior pituitary secretion.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The median eminence is best described as:
a: The highest part of the cerebellum
The lowermost region of the hypothalamus related to the anterior pituitary portal system
A venous sinus behind the pituitary
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The median eminence is the lowermost region of the hypothalamus and is linked to the anterior pituitary portal system.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Growth hormone is also known as:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Growth hormone is also called somatotropic hormone or somatotropin.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Growth hormone acts primarily through which intracellular signaling pathway?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Growth hormone acts through activation of the JAK2-STAT pathway.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: A key gene product activated by growth hormone is:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: IGF-1 is a key gene activated by growth hormone.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following metabolic effects is associated with growth hormone?
a: Decreases fatty acid levels in blood
Increases fatty acid levels in blood
Blocks amino acid transport into cells
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Growth hormone increases fatty acid levels in the blood and promotes their use for energy.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Growth hormone promotes which of the following?
a: Movement of amino acids out of cells
Transfer of amino acids from the cell membrane to the interior
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Growth hormone promotes transfer of amino acids into cells, supporting growth.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Growth hormone stimulates adipose tissue metabolism such that free fatty acids are converted to:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Growth hormone promotes conversion of free fatty acids from adipose tissue into acetyl CoA for energy.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Excess growth hormone before epiphyseal fusion results in:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Growth hormone excess before epiphyseal closure causes gigantism.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Excess growth hormone after epiphyseal fusion results in:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Growth hormone excess after epiphyseal fusion causes acromegaly.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Growth hormone causes the liver to produce:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Growth hormone stimulates the liver to produce somatomedins, also called insulin-like growth factors.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: High levels of IGF-1 are perceived by the hypothalamus to:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: High IGF-1 levels inhibit growth hormone release through negative feedback.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: A patient with adequate growth hormone levels but inability to produce somatomedin C would most likely develop:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Inability to produce somatomedin C can result in dwarfism despite adequate GH levels.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Somatostatin inhibits growth hormone mainly by:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Somatostatin acts by blocking the action of GHRH.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following is a potent stimulator of growth hormone release?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Hypoglycemia and protein deficiency are potent stimulators of GH release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Panhypopituitarism in infants commonly presents as:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: In infants, panhypopituitarism causes dwarfism due to generalized deficiency of anterior pituitary hormones.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following is listed as a common cause of adult-onset panhypopituitarism?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Craniopharyngioma is one of the common causes of adult-onset panhypopituitarism.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which tumor type is listed among the common causes of adult-onset panhypopituitarism?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Chromophobe tumors are listed as a common cause of adult-onset panhypopituitarism.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Postpartum thrombosis of the pituitary blood vessels is a recognized cause of:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Postpartum thrombosis of pituitary blood vessels is a listed cause of adult-onset panhypopituitarism.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The posterior pituitary contains many glial-like cells called:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The posterior pituitary contains glial-like cells called pituicytes.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: The posterior pituitary contains the:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: The posterior pituitary contains the hypothalamic-hypophyseal tract.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: ADH is secreted from which precursor in the hypothalamus?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: ADH is secreted from prepropressophysin in the hypothalamus.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: ADH is secreted together with which carrier protein?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: ADH is secreted with a carrier protein called neurophysin.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Most cases of hereditary hypothalamic diabetes insipidus are linked to a point mutation in:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: A point mutation in neurophysin II underlies most cases of hereditary hypothalamic diabetes insipidus.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: ADH binding to renal tubular cells primarily activates which intracellular messenger?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: ADH binds to surface receptors on renal tubular cells and activates cAMP.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: ADH increases water reabsorption by causing phosphorylation of preformed:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: ADH causes phosphorylation of preformed aquaporin structures.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: ADH causes vasoconstriction by acting on which receptor?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: ADH causes vasoconstriction through V1 receptors.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Oxytocin has which of the following physiologic effects?
a: Causes contraction of the pregnant uterus at term
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Oxytocin causes contraction of the pregnant uterus at term.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Milk letdown or milk ejection is primarily mediated by:
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Oxytocin mediates milk letdown by causing contraction of myoepithelial tissue.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following inhibits oxytocin release or action according to the notes?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Opioids such as endorphins are listed as inhibitory factors for oxytocin.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following stimulates oxytocin release?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Suckling is one of the stimulatory factors for oxytocin release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following is another physiologic stimulus for oxytocin release?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Dilation of the cervix stimulates oxytocin release.

2_pituitary_gland_v2.q: Which of the following is listed as a stimulatory factor for oxytocin release?
2_pituitary_gland_v2.l: Orgasm is listed among the stimulatory factors for oxytocin release.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following cell types in the pancreatic islets primarily secretes insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Beta cells of the pancreatic islets secrete insulin.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following hormones is secreted by alpha cells of the pancreas?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Alpha cells secrete glucagon.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following pancreatic cells secretes somatostatin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Delta cells secrete somatostatin.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: C-peptide is clinically useful because it reflects which of the following?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: C-peptide is released in equimolar amounts with insulin and reflects endogenous insulin production.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: A patient presents with hypoglycemia. Labs show high insulin and low C-peptide. What is the most likely cause?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Low C-peptide with high insulin indicates exogenous insulin use.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: A patient has high insulin and high C-peptide levels during hypoglycemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: High insulin and C-peptide suggests endogenous overproduction (insulinoma).

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Reduction of disulfide bonds in insulin would result in which of the following?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Disulfide bonds are essential for insulin structure and activity.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which glucose transporter is used by pancreatic beta cells to sense glucose levels?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: GLUT2 is a low-affinity, high-capacity transporter used in beta cells.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following transporters is insulin-dependent?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: GLUT4 is insulin-dependent and found in muscle and adipose tissue.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following tissues does NOT require insulin for glucose uptake?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Brain uses GLUT1/3 and is insulin-independent.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: What is the primary intracellular event that triggers insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: ATP closes K+ channels leading to depolarization and insulin release.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Sulfonylurea drugs stimulate insulin secretion by acting on which channel?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Sulfonylureas close ATP-sensitive K+ channels, increasing insulin release.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which ion influx directly triggers insulin exocytosis?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Calcium influx triggers vesicle exocytosis of insulin.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which receptor type does insulin bind to?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin binds to a receptor with intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which subunit of the insulin receptor has tyrosine kinase activity?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Beta subunit has intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Binding of insulin to its receptor leads to which immediate intracellular event?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin binding triggers receptor autophosphorylation.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Insulin promotes glucose uptake in muscle cells by increasing which transporter?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin increases GLUT4 translocation to the membrane.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following is a major metabolic effect of insulin on the liver?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin promotes glycogen synthesis in the liver.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following best describes insulin’s effect on fat metabolism?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin inhibits lipolysis and promotes fat storage.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following is a major protein metabolism effect of insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin promotes protein synthesis and inhibits breakdown.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which electrolyte is driven into cells by insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin promotes potassium uptake into cells.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which hormone has effects antagonistic to insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Growth hormone antagonizes insulin effects.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which state is characterized by increased insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin increases after meals (fed state).

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which incretin hormone enhances insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: GLP-1 increases insulin secretion.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which nervous system input increases insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Parasympathetic stimulation increases insulin secretion.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which condition inhibits insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Sympathetic stimulation via epinephrine inhibits insulin.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which hormone is secreted during hypoglycemia?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Glucagon increases during hypoglycemia.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: What is the main target organ of glucagon?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Glucagon primarily acts on the liver.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Glucagon exerts its effects via which signaling pathway?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Glucagon acts via Gs → cAMP pathway.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which hormone is known as a "hormone of plenty"?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin is released when nutrients are abundant.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which effect occurs within seconds of insulin binding?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin rapidly inserts GLUT4 transporters into membranes.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which effect of insulin occurs over hours to days?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Long-term insulin effects include gene transcription.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which condition results from insulin deficiency in fetus?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin deficiency leads to low birth weight.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which condition results from hyperinsulinemia in fetus?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Excess insulin causes fetal overgrowth (macrosomia).

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which enzyme activity is inhibited by insulin?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin inhibits hormone-sensitive lipase, reducing lipolysis.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which process is stimulated by insulin in adipose tissue?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Insulin promotes fat storage (lipogenesis).

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which transporter is found in RBCs?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: RBCs use GLUT1 for glucose uptake.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which transporter is high affinity and found in brain?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: GLUT3 is high-affinity and used in brain tissue.

3_endocrine_pancreas.q: Which of the following increases insulin secretion?
3_endocrine_pancreas.l: Amino acids stimulate insulin secretion.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which zone of the adrenal cortex primarily secretes mineralocorticoids?
4_adrenal_gland.l: Zona glomerulosa secretes mineralocorticoids such as aldosterone.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which adrenal cortical zone is responsible for glucocorticoid secretion?
4_adrenal_gland.l: Zona fasciculata secretes glucocorticoids like cortisol.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which adrenal cortical zone produces androgens?
Which hormone primarily regulates cortisol secretion?
4_adrenal_gland.l: ACTH controls cortisol and adrenal androgen secretion.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which hormone primarily regulates aldosterone secretion?
4_adrenal_gland.l: Angiotensin II is the main regulator of aldosterone.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which condition directly stimulates aldosterone secretion?
What is the rate-limiting step in steroid hormone synthesis?
4_adrenal_gland.l: Conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone is rate-limiting.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which enzyme converts cortisol to cortisone in mineralocorticoid target tissues?
Which type of receptor does cortisol bind to?
What is the primary effect of aldosterone on the kidney?
Which electrolyte is secreted in response to aldosterone?
Which acid-base effect is associated with aldosterone?
Which steroid class has 21 carbons?
4_adrenal_gland.l: C21 steroids include glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which steroid class is derived from C19 structure?
Which steroid class is derived from C18 structure?
Which urinary marker reflects cortisol secretion?
Which urinary marker reflects androgen secretion?
Which hormone shows diurnal variation with peak in the morning?
What is the effect of cortisol on glucose metabolism?
Which condition is associated with prolonged cortisol elevation?
Which adrenal hormone has anti-inflammatory effects?
Which adrenal hormone suppresses immune response?
Which effect is seen with excess cortisol?
Which effect is seen in connective tissue with excess cortisol?
Which GI effect is associated with cortisol?
Which hormone increases GFR via vasodilation?
4_adrenal_gland.l: Cortisol increases renal blood flow and GFR.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which neurotransmitter system is affected by cortisol?
Which sleep pattern is associated with excess cortisol?
Which hormone is secreted in response to stress?
Which hormone is increased in hypoglycemia?
Which adrenal hormone increases appetite?
Which pathway mediates angiotensin II action?
Which receptor type does angiotensin II use?
Which condition increases renin release?
Which hormone inhibits aldosterone?
Which condition stimulates aldosterone via volume change?
Which hormone is released in pulsatile fashion?
Which hormone is highest at midnight?
4_adrenal_gland.l: Melatonin peaks at night, cortisol lowest.

4_adrenal_gland.q: Which hormone binds mostly to transcortin?
Which hormone has lower protein binding affinity?
